intro: The biggest real distinction between Checkmk and Last9 is straightforward: Checkmk uses freemium with paid upgrades; Last9 uses pricing from $1150, enterprise plan available. Checkmk sits at freemium with paid upgrades and carries low lock-in, while Last9 sits at pricing from $1150, enterprise plan available and carries low lock-in. Checkmk is a fit when you need mobile — typical scenarios include automated build/test/deploy pipelines. Last9 is aimed at microservices performance monitoring, with workloads like microservices performance monitoring as the common case. The honest trade-off: Checkmk is explicitly not for hobby projects; Last9 is not intended for simple, static projects. On cost specifically, pricing model matters more than sticker price — one workload can make Checkmk cheaper while a different shape flips the answer to Last9. Before committing, model your actual usage against both pricing pages: seat counts, data volumes, and whether the free tier covers real workloads tend to dominate the total bill.
quick_take: Checkmk is for mobile; Last9 is for microservices performance monitoring; decide based on cost model and budget.
verdict_a: Choose Checkmk if your primary workload is mobile and low switching cost matters.
verdict_b: Choose Last9 if your primary workload is microservices performance monitoring and low switching cost matters.

faq.a: Last9 publishes an entry price of $1150; Checkmk's entry price is not in our data, so a definitive comparison is not possible without a quote from Checkmk.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Checkmk to Last9?
faq.a: Migrating from Checkmk to Last9 is generally feasible because Checkmk has low lock-in, so data and configuration are usually portable. Since both tools sit in the same category (Performance Monitoring / APM), the conceptual mapping is closer, but you still need to validate feature parity against your specific workload. Plan migration as a project, not a script: inventory integrations, test on non-production workloads, and keep Checkmk running in parallel until Last9 is verified.

Our data gives Checkmk and Last9 the same developer experience score (4/5), so neither has a clear edge on DX by that measure. The differentiator is usually how each tool fits your workflow — things like CLI quality, docs, local dev loop, and error messages. The safest call is to pilot both on a representative task and see which one gets out of your way.

faq.q: Is Last9 a good alternative to Checkmk?
faq.a: Last9 is in the same category as Checkmk (Performance Monitoring / APM), so it's a reasonable alternative to evaluate. Last9 is aimed at microservices performance monitoring, while Checkmk targets mobile — that difference should drive the switch decision. Note that Last9 is explicitly not for simple, static projects — if that describes your workload, it's not a viable replacement.
